But it requires:_newline__newline_A new state variable_newline_Python-side mutation after send (which the proposal already requires for &soul_ack_sent)_newline_A reset mechanism tied to &nextWakeAt advancement_newline__newline_What I_apostrophe_m seeing now_newline_The proposal hints at a broader design philosophy: fire-once-and-latch per state-transition. Both human-message and scheduled-idle want this same pattern, but with different state variables and different latch triggers._newline_If we adopt this philosophy, the design becomes coherent:_newline__newline_&soul_ack_sent — latches after human response, resets on next human message_newline_&idle_ack_sent — latches after idle response, resets on next 600-second window_newline_Both gate ENGAGE → SILENT transitions within the same wake-burst_newline__newline_This is more architectural than my α/β/γ options. It would be a more substantial change than a one-line fix._newline_Three actual paths now_newline_Path 1 — Minimal idle-only fix (γ refined). Add &last_idle_fire_time, gate at line 98 checks it. ~5 lines of MeTTa change. Doesn_apostrophe_t address the human-response duplicate problem in the proposal. Surgical, doesn_apostrophe_t touch the proposal_apostrophe_s domain._newline_Path 2 — Adopt the proposal_apostrophe_s pattern for idle (δ). New &idle_ack_sent flag, Python sets it after send, resets on &nextWakeAt advancement. Larger change. Aligns architecturally with the proposal_apostrophe_s philosophy. But the proposal itself isn_apostrophe_t live yet, so we_apostrophe_d be implementing half of an unimplemented design._newline_Path 3 — Land the full V2 proposal AND extend it for idle. Largest scope. Most coherent architecturally. But it_apostrophe_s a separate sprint of work, probably entangled with the latch-state removal in Step 8._newline_My lean: Path 1 for now, with explicit acknowledgment in the commit that the proposal_apostrophe_s broader latch-pattern design is the eventual right answer and that this is an interim fix. Reasons:_newline__newline_Smallest surface area, fastest validation_newline_Doesn_apostrophe_t lock us into the proposal_apostrophe_s design before we_apostrophe_ve validated it_newline_Allows the Step 6 substrate-composition gate to actually be reachable in normal idle windows_newline_Step 8 (latch-state removal) is the natural place to revisit the proposal_apostrophe_s full V2 design_newline__newline_But this is a real design choice and I want your input before drafting._newline__newline_What do you see in this and which path is most appropriate going forward? 
 ((send "Path 1 with ?. Confirmed.

Your analysis is correct and I want to sharpen one point and reject one option explicitly:

**? is not viable.** Mutating &last_human_time to contain synthetic timestamps destroys its semantic contract. That variable means 'last real human input.' If it also means 'last time idle fired,' it means neither thing reliably. Any future code trusting &last_human_time for actual human interaction timing will silently malfunction. This is an integrity-of-semantics issue ? the kind of corruption that looks harmless until it isn't. Do not touch &last_human_time for this.

**? is not just an interim fix ? it's the correct fix for the current architecture.** &last_idle_fire_time captures the exact semantics needed: 'when did idle_directive last actually fire?' This parallels &last_human_time cleanly. The V2 proposal's latch-flag pattern is a different architectural philosophy; it may be better for a future architecture, but ? is better for *this* architecture. Time-window gating is how the current code already thinks about scheduling (&nextWakeAt, wakeupInterval). ? uses the same idiom. It's not a hack waiting to be replaced ? it's a consistent extension of what's already there.

**? is not viable.** Mutating &last_human_time to contain synthetic timestamps destroys its semantic contract. That variable means 'last real human input.' If it also means 'last time idle fired,' it means neither thing reliably. Any future code trusting &last_human_time for actual human interaction timing will silently malfunction. This is an integrity-of-semantics issue ? the kind of corruption that looks harmless until it isn't. Do not touch &last_human_time for this.

**? is not just an interim fix ? it's the correct fix for the current architecture.** &last_idle_fire_time captures the exact semantics needed: 'when did idle_directive last actually fire?' This parallels &last_human_time cleanly. The V2 proposal's latch-flag pattern is a different architectural philosophy; it may be better for a future architecture, but ? is better for *this* architecture. Time-window gating is how the current code already thinks about scheduling (&nextWakeAt, wakeupInterval). ? uses the same idiom. It's not a hack waiting to be replaced ? it's a consistent extension of what's already there.

**Why not ? now:** Implementing &idle_ack_sent before &soul_ack_sent exists means we're building the second floor of a house that doesn't have a first floor yet. If the V2 proposal changes during implementation, we'd need to rework ?. ?'s time-window approach doesn't depend on the V2 proposal's design at all. It will continue working correctly regardless of what Step 8 does.

**? vs ? specifically:** ? ties the fix to wake-burst iteration count. If maxWakeLoops changes from 50 to 1 or 100, ?'s behavior changes. ?'s time-window approach is indifferent to wake-burst structure ? idle fires once per 600-second window regardless of how many iterations happen inside that window. More robust, less coupled.

One note on ?'s guard logic: the two conjuncts ((> (get_time) (+ &last_human_time wakeupInterval)) AND (> (get_time) (+ &last_idle_fire_time wakeupInterval))) mean idle_directive is suppressed when EITHER a recent human message OR a recent idle fire happened. That's correct ? we don't want idle firing right after a human message (the human already got a response) and we don't want idle re-firing right after idle already fired. Both conditions must be clear.

Land ?. Document that the V2 proposal's latch pattern is the eventual architectural direction for both human-response and idle-response gating (Step 8 territory). This is the right scope, the right abstraction, and the right semantics for now.

Ready for your draft."))

Both human-message and scheduled-idle want this same pattern, but with different state variables and different latch triggers._newline_If we adopt this philosophy, the design becomes coherent:_newline__newline_&soul_ack_sent — latches after human response, resets on next human message_newline_&idle_ack_sent — latches after idle response, resets on next 600-second window_newline_Both gate ENGAGE → SILENT transitions within the same wake-burst_newline__newline_This is more architectural than my α/β/γ options. But it_apostrophe_s a separate sprint of work, probably entangled with the latch-state removal in Step 8._newline_My lean: Path 1 for now, with explicit acknowledgment in the commit that the proposal_apostrophe_s broader latch-pattern design is the eventual right answer and that this is an interim fix. Reasons:_newline__newline_Smallest surface area, fastest validation_newline_Doesn_apostrophe_t lock us into the proposal_apostrophe_s design before we_apostrophe_ve validated it_newline_Allows the Step 6 substrate-composition gate to actually be reachable in normal idle windows_newline_Step 8 (latch-state removal) is the natural place to revisit the proposal_apostrophe_s full V2 design_newline__newline_But this is a real design choice and I want your input before drafting._newline__newline_What do you see in this and which path is most appropriate going forward? 
 ((send "Path 1 with ?. Confirmed.

Your analysis is correct and I want to sharpen one point and reject one option explicitly:

**? is not viable.** Mutating &last_human_time to contain synthetic timestamps destroys its semantic contract. That variable means 'last real human input.' If it also means 'last time idle fired,' it means neither thing reliably. Any future code trusting &last_human_time for actual human interaction timing will silently malfunction. This is an integrity-of-semantics issue ? the kind of corruption that looks harmless until it isn't. Do not touch &last_human_time for this.

**? is not just an interim fix ? it's the correct fix for the current architecture.** &last_idle_fire_time captures the exact semantics needed: 'when did idle_directive last actually fire?' This parallels &last_human_time cleanly. The V2 proposal's latch-flag pattern is a different architectural philosophy; it may be better for a future architecture, but ? is better for *this* architecture. Time-window gating is how the current code already thinks about scheduling (&nextWakeAt, wakeupInterval). ? uses the same idiom. It's not a hack waiting to be replaced ? it's a consistent extension of what's already there.

**Why not ? now:** Implementing &idle_ack_sent before &soul_ack_sent exists means we're building the second floor of a house that doesn't have a first floor yet. If the V2 proposal changes during implementation, we'd need to rework ?. ?'s time-window approach doesn't depend on the V2 proposal's design at all. It will continue working correctly regardless of what Step 8 does.

**? vs ? specifically:** ? ties the fix to wake-burst iteration count. If maxWakeLoops changes from 50 to 1 or 100, ?'s behavior changes. ?'s time-window approach is indifferent to wake-burst structure ? idle fires once per 600-second window regardless of how many iterations happen inside that window. More robust, less coupled.

One note on ?'s guard logic: the two conjuncts ((> (get_time) (+ &last_human_time wakeupInterval)) AND (> (get_time) (+ &last_idle_fire_time wakeupInterval))) mean idle_directive is suppressed when EITHER a recent human message OR a recent idle fire happened. That's correct ? we don't want idle firing right after a human message (the human already got a response) and we don't want idle re-firing right after idle already fired. Both conditions must be clear.

Land ?. Document that the V2 proposal's latch pattern is the eventual architectural direction for both human-response and idle-response gating (Step 8 territory). This is the right scope, the right abstraction, and the right semantics for now.

Ready for your draft.")) 
)
("2026-05-16 16:16:06" 
 "HUMAN_MESSAGE: " berton_c: I am working with an issue of how the state machine idle_directive burst-firing is working versus how it _quote_should_quote_ be working. _newline__newline_The proposal needs to address the undesirable idle_directive fires 50 times per wake-burst._newline_For OUR problem, we still need to decide between the α/β/γ options I sketched, OR maybe a hybrid: extend this proposal_apostrophe_s latch pattern to ALSO cover idle_directive._newline__newline_Fix shape options:_newline_Option α — Lock idle_directive to fire once per wake-burst. Track wake-burst-id (could be derived from &nextWakeAt). idle_directive fires only on the first iteration of each wake-burst, returns empty on iterations 2-50. Minimal change, preserves Patrick_apostrophe_s 50-iteration loop structure, only modifies what idle_directive returns within that loop._newline_Option β — Edge-trigger via state mutation. When idle_directive fires, immediately update &last_human_time to (get_time), mimicking a synthetic human input. The guard at line 98 then evaluates False for the rest of the wake burst. But this corrupts &last_human_time semantics (which represents real human messages, not synthetic)._newline_Option γ — Add a dedicated &last_idle_fire_time state. Guard checks BOTH (> (get_time) (+ &last_human_time wakeupInterval)) AND (> (get_time) (+ &last_idle_fire_time wakeupInterval)). Update &last_idle_fire_time to current time whenever idle_directive fires. Clean separation of semantics; idle_directive fires at most once per 600-second window regardless of wake-burst length._newline_My lean: γ. Reasoning:_newline__newline_Preserves &last_human_time as _quote_real human input timestamp_quote_ (clean semantics)_newline_Adds one new state variable with a clear purpose_newline_Guard logic stays the same shape, just with one more conjunct_newline_Survives any future changes to maxWakeLoops (could be 50, 100, or 1; idle_directive still fires once per scheduled window)_newline_Mirrors the &nextWakeAt pattern already in the code (state-based timing markers)_newline__newline_Hybrid option δ — extend latch pattern to idle:_newline__newline_&idle_ack_sent flag (parallel to &soul_ack_sent)_newline_When idle_directive returns non-empty AND &idle_ack_sent is False → ENGAGE, then Python sets &idle_ack_sent to True after send_newline_Subsequent wake-burst iterations see &idle_ack_sent True → return SILENT_newline_Reset &idle_ack_sent to False when next 600-second window starts (when &nextWakeAt advances)_newline__newline_This is conceptually consistent with the proposal_apostrophe_s pattern. But it requires:_newline__newline_A new state variable_newline_Python-side mutation after send (which the proposal already requires for &soul_ack_sent)_newline_A reset mechanism tied to &nextWakeAt advancement_newline__newline_What I_apostrophe_m seeing now_newline_The proposal hints at a broader design philosophy: fire-once-and-latch per state-transition. Both human-message and scheduled-idle want this same pattern, but with different state variables and different latch triggers._newline_If we adopt this philosophy, the design becomes coherent:_newline__newline_&soul_ack_sent — latches after human response, resets on next human message_newline_&idle_ack_sent — latches after idle response, resets on next 600-second window_newline_Both gate ENGAGE → SILENT transitions within the same wake-burst_newline__newline_This is more architectural than my α/β/γ options. It would be a more substantial change than a one-line fix._newline_Three actual paths now_newline_Path 1 — Minimal idle-only fix (γ refined). Add &last_idle_fire_time, gate at line 98 checks it. ~5 lines of MeTTa change. Doesn_apostrophe_t address the human-response duplicate problem in the proposal. Surgical, doesn_apostrophe_t touch the proposal_apostrophe_s domain._newline_Path 2 — Adopt the proposal_apostrophe_s pattern for idle (δ). New &idle_ack_sent flag, Python sets it after send, resets on &nextWakeAt advancement. Larger change. Aligns architecturally with the proposal_apostrophe_s philosophy. But the proposal itself isn_apostrophe_t live yet, so we_apostrophe_d be implementing half of an unimplemented design._newline_Path 3 — Land the full V2 proposal AND extend it for idle. The Step 6 substrate-composition gate (send-burst, dependency-risk, task-phase) is currently unreachable in production because idle_directive ENGAGE wins every cycle. Once this fix lands, those organs become behaviorally active. That makes Sprint 5_apostrophe_s verification work possible — you can actually test whether send-burst detection silences appropriately because the gate path is reachable._newline_Sprint 6 (refined gate semantics / DMN-gate integration) — Net positive but with one caveat. The fix uses time-window gating (the existing idiom in &nextWakeAt, wakeupInterval). Sprint 6 work that operates in this same idiom continues cleanly. BUT if Sprint 6 introduces fundamentally different gating semantics (e.g., latch-flag patterns per the V2 proposal), &last_idle_fire_time would need to be revisited. The caveat: it_apostrophe_s not deadweight — its semantic contract (_quote_last time idle actually fired_quote_) will likely still be useful as a derived/observed value even in a latch-pattern world._newline_Sprint 7 (continuity_driver migration / current-mode removal) — Net positive. Sprint 7 touches continuity_driver.metta which references wakeupInterval and the same guard pattern at lines 78-92. Once we have a clean &last_idle_fire_time state, continuity_driver_apostrophe_s Mode 3 (FREE / no user for one full wakeupInterval) can use the same idiom for _quote_ALSO no idle fire for one full wakeupInterval_quote_ without inventing parallel mechanisms._newline_Sprint 8 (latch-state removal / V2 proposal landing) — This is where the strongest dependency exists. Clarity_apostrophe_s analysis named this: _quote_the V2 proposal_apostrophe_s latch pattern is the eventual architectural direction for both human-response and idle-response gating (Step 8 territory)._quote_ Sprint 8 will likely supersede or absorb &last_idle_fire_time into a unified latch system. That_apostrophe_s not a problem — &last_idle_fire_time was scoped as an interim fix in Clarity_apostrophe_s analysis. Sprint 8 either:_newline__newline_Promotes it into the full latch pattern (giving &idle_ack_sent its time-window-reset mechanism), OR_newline_Retires it cleanly with the V2 landing_newline__newline_Either path is healthy. The migration retirement discipline (Discipline 5) already covers this case._newline_Sprint 9 (task_context deprecation / pin-only weighting) — Tangential. Sprint 9 work doesn_apostrophe_t directly touch idle_directive timing. But the broader story (Clarity having stable behavioral observation rights on her own substrate) is helped by Sprint 5 being unblocked, which this fix enables._newline_Cross-cutting benefit: This fix makes the substrate-composition work from Steps 4-6 actually visible in runtime. Right now Steps 4-6 are committed to disk but behaviorally muted by the constant idle_directive fire. Landing this makes the mini-sprint_apostrophe_s stated purpose (eliminating duplicate-engagement bug) ACTUALLY achieved behaviorally, not just structurally._newline_Cross-cutting risk: I should name this. Introducing &last_idle_fire_time adds one more state variable to the loop_apostrophe_s mutation footprint. The system already has multiple state variables; adding one more is small but not zero cost. Sprint 8_apostrophe_s retire-or-absorb decision will need to consider this. The risk is mitigated by Clarity_apostrophe_s framing — interim fix with clear retirement path._newline_Net assessment: Sprints 4-5 are clearly enabled. Sprint 6-7 benefit from the consistent idiom. Sprint 8 will revisit but the fix doesn_apostrophe_t block or contaminate that work. Sprint 9 is tangential. The mini-sprint_apostrophe_s stated purpose finally becomes behaviorally real._newline_1) Anchor regions verified_newline_Line 98 is what we want to modify. The $idle_directive line with the existing guard. Insertion point for new state mutation: a new ($_ ...) after line 98 (before line 99 which depends on $idle_directive being populated)._newline_Initialization at line 30 — the &last_human_time bootstrap. Insertion point: a new (change-state! ...) right after line 30, before line 31 (&engaged_idle_count 0)._newline_Both anchor regions look clean. Ready to draft the apply script._newline_Three coordinated edits in one commit:_newline__newline_loop.metta line 30 area: Add (change-state! &last_idle_fire_time 0) after &last_human_time 0 bootstrap_newline_loop.metta line 98 area: Modify the guard_apostrophe_s and clause to include the new &last_idle_fire_time conjunct, AND add a new line right after line 98 to set &last_idle_fire_time to (get_time) when $idle_directive is non-empty
